critique.summary: The article critiques the claim that detailed specification documents can fully replace code generation, using OpenAI's Symphony project as a case study. It argues that specifications often resemble code themselves, and attempts to generate working code purely from specs are unreliable and flawed.
mainPoints: Agentic coding advocates mistakenly believe specification documents are simpler than code and that specification work is more thoughtful than coding.
The Symphony project’s specification document is essentially pseudocode or code in markdown form, undermining the claim that specs can substitute for code.
Generating reliable code from specifications is difficult and often fails, as demonstrated by the author's unsuccessful attempt to generate Symphony code using an AI agent.
Specifications that are detailed enough to generate code tend to become as complex as code, negating the supposed simplicity advantage.
The quality of specification documents is often compromised by industry pressures to optimize for speed over clarity and coherence, resulting in 'sloppy' specs.
The principle of 'garbage in, garbage out' applies: unclear or incomplete specifications cannot reliably produce correct code via AI agents.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims are largely accurate and supported by concrete examples, including the author's own experiment with AI-generated code from the Symphony spec and references to well-known challenges in specification adherence such as YAML. The critique aligns with established software engineering understanding that precise specifications often become as complex as code and that AI code generation from natural language specs remains unreliable. However, the article is based on a single project and personal experimentation, and does not cite multiple independent sources or perspectives, limiting its comprehensiveness.
considerations: The article does not explore successful cases or methodologies where specifications have effectively driven code generation, such as formal methods or domain-specific languages.
It does not consider ongoing advances in AI and natural language processing that may improve code generation reliability in the future.
The discussion focuses on one project and one AI tool, which may not represent the broader landscape of specification-driven development or AI coding agents.
The article assumes that specification documents must be fully detailed and formalized to be useful for code generation, potentially overlooking incremental or hybrid approaches.
The impact of human oversight and iterative refinement in AI-assisted coding workflows is not addressed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the idea that a sufficiently detailed specification can be considered code, with varying opinions on the practicality and implications of this view. Some commenters argue that specifications and code serve different purposes and that AI's role in interpreting or generating code complicates traditional distinctions.

comments.author: causalityltd
comments.text:
The cognitive dissonance comes from the tension between the-spec-as-management-artifact vs the-spec-as-engineering-artifact. Author is right that advocates are selling the first but second is the only one which works.

For a manager, the spec exists in order to create a delgation ticket, something you assign to someone and done. But for a builder, it exists as a thinking tool that evolves with the code to sharpen the understanding/thinking.

I also think, that some builders are being fooled into thinking like managers because ease, but they figure it out pretty quickly.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully distinguishes between the managerial and engineering perspectives on specs, highlighting a key source of confusion and aligning well with software development realities.

comments.author: quotemstr
comments.text:
No, a spec is not code. It's possible to describe simple behavior that's nevertheless difficult to implement. Consider, say,

  fn sin(x: f16) -> f16
There are only 64k different f16s. Easy enough to test them all. A given sin() is either correct or it's not.

Yet sin() here can have a large number of different implementations. The spec alone under-determines the actual code.
comments.analysis: The comment correctly points out that specifications can underdetermine implementations, showing that specs and code are distinct even if closely related.

comments.author: hintymad
comments.text:
> A sufficiently detailed spec is code

This is exactly the argument in Brooks' No Silver Bullet. I still believe that it holds. However, my observation is that many people don't really need that level of details. When one prompts an AI to "write me a to-do list app", what they really mean is that "write me a to-do list app that is better that I have imagined so far", which does not really require detailed spec.
comments.analysis: The comment references a well-known software engineering principle and pragmatically notes that detailed specs may not always be necessary, especially in AI-assisted development.

comments.author: ranyume
comments.text: I tried myself to make a language over an agent's prompt. This programing language is interpreted in real time, and parts of it are deterministic and parts are processed by an LLM. It's possible, but I think that it's hard to code anything in such a language. This is because when we think of code we make associations that the LLM doesn't make and we handle data that the LLM might ignore entirely. Worse, the LLM understands certain words differently than us and the LLM has limited expressions because of it's limits in true reasoning (LLMs can only express a limited number of ideas, thus a limited number of correct outputs).
comments.analysis: This comment provides a practical perspective on the limits of LLMs in coding languages, emphasizing challenges in reasoning and deterministic behavior.

comments.author: kikkupico
comments.text: Natural language is fluid and ambiguous while code is rigid and deterministic. Spec-driven development appears to be the best of both worlds. But really, it is the worst of both. LLMs are language models - their breakthrough capability is handling natural language. Code is meant to be unambiguous and deterministic. A spec is neither fluid nor deterministic.
comments.analysis: The comment critically examines the nature of specifications and code, highlighting the inherent contradictions in spec-driven development.

comments.author: scuff3d
comments.text:
I recently left this comment on another thread. At the time I was focused on planning mode, but it applies here.

Plan mode is a trap. It makes you feel like you're actually engineering a solution. Like you're making measured choices about implementation details. You're not, your just vibe coding with extra steps. I come from an electrical engineering background originally, and I've worked in aerospace most of my career. Most software devs don't know what planning is. The mechanical, electrical, and aerospace engineering teams plan for literal years. Countless reviews and re-reviews, trade studies, down selects, requirement derivations, MBSE diagrams, and God knows what else before anything that will end up in the final product is built. It's meticulous, detailed, time consuming work, and bloody expensive.

That's the world software engineering has been trying to leave behind for at least two decades, and now with LLMs people think they can move back to it with a weekend of "planning", answering a handful of questions, and a task list.

Even if LLMs could actually execute on a spec to the degree people claim (they can't), it would take as long to properly define as it would to just write it with AI assistance in the first place.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a valuable comparison between software planning and traditional engineering disciplines, cautioning against oversimplified planning with LLMs.

comments.author: measurablefunc
comments.text: I agree with the overall structure of the argument but I like to think of specifications like polynomial equations defining some set of zeroes. Specifications are not really code but a good specification will cut out a definable subset of expected behaviors that can then be further refined with an executable implementation. For example, if a specification calls for a lock-free queue then there are any number of potential implementations w/ different trade-offs that I would not expect to be in the specification.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced analogy comparing specs to polynomial equations, clarifying the relationship between specs and implementations.

comments.author: sjeiuhvdiidi
comments.text: Absolute nonsense. A sufficiently detailed "spec" is the code. What is wrong with you people ? Pure nonsense, all they have to offer.
comments.analysis: This comment is dismissive without providing substantive reasoning, reducing its credibility and usefulness in the discussion.

comments.author: Panzerschrek
comments.text: I am developing my own programming language, but I have no specification written for it. When people tell me that I need a specification, I reply that I already have one - the source code of the language compiler.
comments.analysis: This comment illustrates a practical approach where source code serves as the de facto specification, highlighting a real-world perspective.

comments.author: rdevilla
comments.text:
I think it's only a matter of time before people start trying to optimize model performance and token usage by creating their own more technical dialect of English (LLMSpeak or something). It will reduce both ambiguity and token usage by using a highly compressed vocabulary, where very precise concepts are packed into single words (monads are just monoids in the category of endofunctors, what's the problem?). Grammatically, expect things like the Oxford comma to emerge that reduce ambiguity and rounds of back-and-forth clarification with the agent.

The uninitiated can continue trying to clumsily refer to the same concepts, but with 100x the tokens, as they lack the same level of precision in their prompting. Anyone wanting to maximize their LLM productivity will start speaking in this unambiguous, highly information-dense dialect that optimizes their token usage and LLM spend...
comments.analysis: This comment speculates on future developments in prompt engineering, presenting a plausible scenario for increased precision and efficiency.
